claim: c-MYC is important for maintaining pluripotent state of pluripotent stem cells.

result.claim: c-MYC is important for maintaining pluripotent state of pluripotent stem cells.
result.reasoning: Multiple lines of evidence support the claim that c-MYC is important for maintaining the pluripotent state of pluripotent stem cells. Paper ID 22843616 explicitly states that "Myc sustains pluripotency through repression of the primitive endoderm master regulator GATA6." Furthermore, Paper ID 25300426 indicates that "Maintained expression of stable Myc (T58A) renders self-renewal and maintenance of pluripotency independent of LIF," and that LIF/STAT3, which controls ES cell self-renewal and pluripotency, does so by a Myc-dependent mechanism, with STAT3 directly regulating c-Myc expression. Paper ID 11900693 highlights that "The pluripotency transcriptional network in embryonic stem cells (ESCs) is composed of distinct functional units including the core and Myc units," and that "The function of Nac1 in promoting ESC self-renewal appears to be partially mediated by c-Myc." Additionally, c-Myc is recognized as one of the four transcription factors (Oct4, Sox2, c-Myc, and Klf4) whose ectopic expression is sufficient to confer a pluripotent state upon the fibroblast genome, generating induced pluripotent stem cells (Paper ID 21271817). While Paper ID 2002182 notes that c-Myc is "dispensable" for iPS cell generation, it also states that "in its absence the reprogramming efficiency is reduced," indicating its significant importance even if not strictly essential in all scenarios. The collective evidence strongly points to c-MYC's crucial role in pluripotency maintenance and self-renewal.

evidence.text: Continuous proliferation of MafB/c-Maf deficient macrophages requires concomitant up-regulation of KLF4 and c-Myc.
evidence.source: MafB/c-Maf deficiency enables self-renewal of differentiated functional macrophages.

evidence.text: Patients with c-MET overexpression had a significantly shorter overall survival of 27 months.
evidence.source: c‐MET Overexpression in Colorectal Cancer: A Poor Prognostic Factor for Survival

evidence.text: The reprogramming efficiency is reduced in the absence of c-Myc.
evidence.source: A Combined Chemical and Genetic Approach for the Generation of Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells

evidence.text: MafB and c-Maf deficiency enables extended expansion of mature monocytes and macrophages in culture.
evidence.source: MafB/c-Maf deficiency enables self-renewal of differentiated functional macrophages.

evidence.text: Induced pluripotent stem (iPS) cells were generated from fibroblasts.
evidence.source: Directly reprogrammed fibroblasts show global epigenetic remodeling and widespread tissue contribution.

evidence.text: Caprin1 upregulates Ccnd2 and c-Myc.
evidence.source: MVP-mediated exosomal sorting of miR-193a promotes colon cancer progression

evidence.text: c-Myc is one of the four genes originally thought to be required for iPS cell generation.
evidence.source: A Combined Chemical and Genetic Approach for the Generation of Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells

evidence.text: Nac1 directly binds to the c-Myc promoter and regulates c-Myc transcription.
evidence.source: Nac1 promotes self-renewal of embryonic stem cells through direct transcriptional regulation of c-Myc

evidence.text: Maintained expression of stable Myc (T58A) renders self-renewal and maintenance of pluripotency independent of LIF.
evidence.source: LIF/STAT3 controls ES cell self-renewal and pluripotency by a Myc-dependent mechanism.

evidence.text: The function of Nac1 in promoting ESC self-renewal appears to be partially mediated by c-Myc.
evidence.source: Nac1 promotes self-renewal of embryonic stem cells through direct transcriptional regulation of c-Myc

evidence.text: Myc sustains pluripotency through repression of the primitive endoderm master regulator GATA6.
evidence.source: Myc represses primitive endoderm differentiation in pluripotent stem cells.

evidence.text: Patients with c-MET overexpression had a significantly shorter overall survival of 39 months.
evidence.source: c‐MET Overexpression in Colorectal Cancer: A Poor Prognostic Factor for Survival

evidence.text: The pluripotency transcriptional network in embryonic stem cells (ESCs) is composed of distinct functional units including the core and Myc units.
evidence.source: Nac1 promotes self-renewal of embryonic stem cells through direct transcriptional regulation of c-Myc

evidence.text: Proteomic and genomic approaches have identified Nac1 as a member of the core pluripotency network.

evidence.text: Murine ES cells can be maintained as a pluripotent, self-renewing population by LIF/STAT3-dependent signaling.
evidence.source: LIF/STAT3 controls ES cell self-renewal and pluripotency by a Myc-dependent mechanism.

evidence.text: The effects of SOD1 on CK1γ stability are observed in a human cell line.
evidence.source: SOD1 Integrates Signals from Oxygen and Glucose to Repress Respiration

evidence.text: In this study, the role of GEF-H1 in complement-induced RhoA activation was explored.
evidence.source: Role of Guanine Nucleotide Exchange Factor-H1 in Complement-mediated RhoA Activation in Glomerular Epithelial Cells

evidence.text: Ectopic expression of Oct4, Sox2, c-Myc, and Klf4 is sufficient to confer a pluripotent state upon the fibroblast genome.

evidence.text: Nac1 is a self-renewal promoting factor, but is not required for maintaining pluripotency of ESCs.

evidence.text: c-MET overexpression was detected in 39 colorectal cancer (CRC) patients.
